nameMeanings.name: Jacobsen
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Lars, Jorgen, Nils, Anders, Gudrun, Iver, Knud, Alf, Bjorn, Borge, Fredrik.
Danish, Norwegian, North German, and Dutch: patronymic from the personal name   Jacob  . Compare   Jacobson   and   Jakobsen  .

variants.name: Jacob
meanings: Jewish, English, Welsh, German, Portuguese, French (mainly Alsace and Lorraine), Walloon, Breton, Dutch, Flemish, Swedish, Danish, and Norwegian: derivative, via Latin Jacob(us), from the Hebrew personal name Ya‘aqob (Yaakov). In the Bible, this is the name of the crafty younger twin brother of Esau (Genesis 25:26), who took advantage of the latter's hunger and impetuousness to persuade him to part with his birthright ‘for a mess of potage’. The name is traditionally interpreted as coming from Hebrew akev ‘heel’: Jacob is said to have been born holding on to Esau's heel. In English usage the name Jacob is regarded as distinct from the name James , but they are of identical origin. In North America, the English form of the surname has absorbed cognates from other languages, for example Assyrian/Chaldean or Arabic Yaqub , Yakub , Yacoub , or Yacob , Slovenian Jakob and Jakop, Czech and Slovak Jakub , and also their patronymics and other derivatives (see examples at Jacobs and Jacobson ). The name Jacob is also found among Christians in southern India (compare Chacko ), but since South Indians traditionally do not have hereditary surnames, the southern Indian name was in most cases registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.

nameMeanings.name: Peder
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: form of the name of the best-known of all Christ's apostles, traditionally regarded as the founder of the Christian Church. The name derives, via Latin, from Greek petros ‘stone, rock’. This is used as a translation of the Aramaic byname Cephas, given to the apostle Simon son of Jona, to distinguish him from another of the same name ( Simon Zelotes ). ‘When Jesus beheld him, he said, Thou art Simon the son of Jona: thou shalt be called Cephas, which is by interpretation, A stone’ (John 1:42). According to Matthew 16:17–18, Christ says more explicitly, ‘Blessed art thou, Simon Bar-jona … thou art Peter, and upon this rock I will build my church’. The name has been in continuous use since the Middle Ages.

en.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram was born about 1275, in Germany as the son of Jacob Pedersen Skram and Ukendt. He had at least 11 sons and 2 daughters with Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee.

fr.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram est né environ 1275, à Allemagne, fils de Jacob Pedersen Skram et Ukendt. Il a eu au moins 11 fils et 2 filles avec Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee.

de.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram wurde um 1275 in Deutschland, als Sohn von Jacob Pedersen Skram und Ukendt geboren. Er hatte mit Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee mindestens 11 Söhne und 2 Töchter.

it.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram è nato il circa 1275 nel Germania come figlio di Jacob Pedersen Skram e Ukendt. Lui avuto almeno 11 figli e 2 figlie con Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee.

ko.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram(은)는 대략 1275년 독일에서 Jacob Pedersen Skram(와)과 Ukendt의 아들로 태어났다. 그는 Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee(와)과 적어도 열 명 이상의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다.

pt.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram nasceu aproximadamente 1275, em Alemanha filho de Jacob Pedersen Skram e Ukendt. Ele teve pelo menos 11 filhos e 2 filhas com Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee.

ru.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram родился около 1275 г. в местности Германия. Его родителей звали Jacob Pedersen Skram и Ukendt. У него было по крайней мере 11 сыновей и 2 дочери от Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee.

es.lifeSummary: Peder Jacobsen Skram nació aproximadamente 1275, en Alemania como hijo de Jacob Pedersen Skram y Ukendt. Tuvo por lo menos 11 hijos y 2 hijas con Edel Pedersdatter Saltensee.
